title: Case Study: Building a Robust File Reader

questions.objective_ref: Explain why a real-world data reader must skip and log bad rows instead of crashing on the first one.
questions.feedback: A robust reader keeps going: it processes what it can, sets aside what it can't, and finishes the file rather than stopping at the first defect.

questions.objective_ref: Explain why a real-world data reader must skip and log bad rows instead of crashing on the first one.
questions.feedback: The failure is usually in the data, not the code, so keep-going preserves the thousands of good rows and reports the few bad ones.

questions.objective_ref: Structure a read loop so a try/except wraps the processing of each individual row, isolating one bad row from the rest.
questions.feedback: Placing the try/except inside the loop sends a row's failure to that iteration's handler and lets the loop continue with the next row.

questions.objective_ref: Structure a read loop so a try/except wraps the processing of each individual row, isolating one bad row from the rest.
questions.feedback: Wrapping the whole loop from the outside is the fragile version: the first bad row breaks out and every remaining row is lost.

questions.objective_ref: Validate a row's fields — presence, type, and range — and raise a clear error when a row is invalid.
questions.feedback: CSV values are strings, so a type error surfaces at int() conversion, which raises ValueError on non-numeric text.

questions.objective_ref: Validate a row's fields — presence, type, and range — and raise a clear error when a row is invalid.
questions.feedback: int("-5") succeeds, so a range/business-rule failure is one you must detect and raise yourself with a clear ValueError.

questions.objective_ref: Validate a row's fields — presence, type, and range — and raise a clear error when a row is invalid.
questions.feedback: Catch the specific expected errors — ValueError, KeyError, AttributeError — rather than a broad or bare except that also hides real bugs.

questions.objective_ref: Separate valid records from invalid ones, returning clean data in one collection and an error report in another.
questions.feedback: Two collections keep clean data usable by downstream code and rejects readable by a human, so the two are never mixed.

questions.objective_ref: Separate valid records from invalid ones, returning clean data in one collection and an error report in another.
questions.feedback: Mixing good and bad rows means callers can no longer tell them apart and may silently process garbage.

questions.objective_ref: Assemble file opening, CSV parsing, per-row validation, and error collection into one complete, runnable reader function.
questions.feedback: enumerate(reader, start=2) numbers rows from 2 because line 1 is the header, so error messages point at the real file line.

questions.objective_ref: Assemble file opening, CSV parsing, per-row validation, and error collection into one complete, runnable reader function.
questions.feedback: A short row does not raise on its own; DictReader fills the missing field with restval (default None), which validation must catch.

questions.objective_ref: Write the clean rows back out to a new CSV so downstream code only ever sees good data.
questions.feedback: csv.DictWriter with writeheader() then writerows(valid) produces a clean output file containing only validated rows.

questions.objective_ref: Explain why a real-world data reader must skip and log bad rows instead of crashing on the first one.
questions.feedback: The skip-and-log pattern rejects a bad row with a recorded reason, then continues to the next row.

questions.objective_ref: Separate valid records from invalid ones, returning clean data in one collection and an error report in another.
questions.feedback: Never fail silently: a skipped row must be recorded (collected or printed), because losing data quietly is worse than crashing.
